coredata.dc:title: Clustering of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots using biopolymers for biomedical applications
coredata.dc:creator: Emam, Hossam E.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel member in the family of multifunctional carbon-nanostructures that comprising distinctive characters, has the potentiality for revolutionization in different important purposes for supporting the transformation to sustainable bioeconomy, as it becomes obligatory object for traceable target delivering, biomedical purposes, and various therapeutical applications. CQDs are advantageous with different attractive characters, like, interchangeable optoelectronics and photoluminescence properties, low toxic effects, biocompatibility, biological activities and high-water solubility. Diverse methodologies and precursor molecules were applied for synthesizing of CQDs. The opportunity for scaling-up the preparation of CQDs via obeying the basic concepts of green chemistry is a recent researching hotspot and a prerequisite for wide-scaled production and applicability. Carbohydrate polymers are readily naturally occurring/abundant chiral biomolecular structures that could be demonstrated as an extensively attractive and cost-effective starting materials from which optically active CQDs with distinct properties and interesting applicability could be successfully nucleated. Herein, the different biological-based carbon sources as precursor molecules of CQDs, photoluminescence of CQDs, biodistribution and pharmaco-kinetic studies, cytotoxicity and the biomedical applications of CQDs (sensing and transfecting properties, drug delivery and bone tissue engineering) were briefly presented. The objective of the presented review is to consolidate the recent reports in literature concerned with synthesis of CQDs from different carbohydrate polymers and their applicability in biomedical purposes, including the discussed explanations and interpretation on the current challenging. It could be eventually summarized that, the opportunity in tuning the synthetic techniques for synthesis of CQDs renders these nanostructures amenable to numerous applications.

head.ce:title: Clustering of photoluminescent carbon quantum dots using biopolymers for biomedical applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a novel member in the family of multifunctional carbon-nanostructures that comprising distinctive characters, has the potentiality for revolutionization in different important purposes for supporting the transformation to sustainable bioeconomy, as it becomes obligatory object for traceable target delivering, biomedical purposes, and various therapeutical applications. CQDs are advantageous with different attractive characters, like, interchangeable optoelectronics and photoluminescence properties, low toxic effects, biocompatibility, biological activities and high-water solubility. Diverse methodologies and precursor molecules were applied for synthesizing of CQDs. The opportunity for scaling-up the preparation of CQDs via obeying the basic concepts of green chemistry is a recent researching hotspot and a prerequisite for wide-scaled production and applicability. Carbohydrate polymers are readily naturally occurring/abundant chiral biomolecular structures that could be demonstrated as an extensively attractive and cost-effective starting materials from which optically active CQDs with distinct properties and interesting applicability could be successfully nucleated. Herein, the different biological-based carbon sources as precursor molecules of CQDs, photoluminescence of CQDs, biodistribution and pharmaco-kinetic studies, cytotoxicity and the biomedical applications of CQDs (sensing and transfecting properties, drug delivery and bone tissue engineering) were briefly presented. The objective of the presented review is to consolidate the recent reports in literature concerned with synthesis of CQDs from different carbohydrate polymers and their applicability in biomedical purposes, including the discussed explanations and interpretation on the current challenging. It could be eventually summarized that, the opportunity in tuning the synthetic techniques for synthesis of CQDs renders these nanostructures amenable to numerous applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive consideration with health, energy and environment has contentiously encouraged the researchers for investigation of novel/unique reagents and technologies for conservation of energy, boosting sustainable ecosystems and promoting human well-beings (Julien et al., 2017). Carbon nanostructures are categorized as unique class of novel structures with high potentiality for positive influencing both society and science. However, most of the reported synthetic techniques for nanostructures usually require fossil fuel-based raw materials, toxic chemicals and high energy consumption that are against the main concepts of green chemistry (Sangam et al., 2018). Green synthetic techniques and exploitation of biocompatible/renewable sources are required for releasing the full potency of such materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are subcategorized and ascribed as a promising type of carbon nanostructures, to extensively attract the attention since it was investigated in 2004 within separating and purifying of carbon nanotubes with single-wall (X. Xu et al., 2004). CQDs are initially described as quasi-spherical nano-objects particle size ranged in 1–10 nm (Sun et al., 2006). In accordance to the carbonic core composition and decorative functional groups, CQDs could be categorized into graphene or graphene oxide quantum dots (GQDs or GOQDs) and carbonized polymeric based dots (CPDs) (S. Zhu et al., 2015).

Owing to the quantum size effects, the state surface defect, and the transitions of band gap of the conjugated π-dominations, CQDs exhibit tunable and strong optical properties, superior up-conversion luminescence, excellence in the photostability and the performance of electro-excited charge transferring (Z. L. Wu, Liu and Yuan, 2017). These lead to their superior applicability in the different purposes such as in sensing, as optronics, in energy storing and as photocatalysts. CQDs with controllable size can be exploited in the biomedical applications like their application as bio-sensors (Y. Song et al., 2018), in bio-imaging (X. Feng et al., 2015), in bio-labeling (P. Das, Bose, Das, Banerjee and Das, 2018), drug delivery (Gedda et al., 2016), metal ions detection in water (Boruah et al., 2020; T. Das et al., 2019) and as nonfertilizer (Saikia et al., 2022). These could be attributed to good biocompatibility, fluorescence, low toxicity, and hydrophilicity. It was also shown that CQDs could act as bioactive booster to motivate the cell growing or mineralizing on the scaffold compounds (Hassanzadeh et al., 2016; D. Wu, Samanta, Srivastava and Hakkarainen, 2017). Additionally, the wide surface area and advantageous surficial functionality as well as enriching of π-domains acted in the allowance of CQDs to be effectively interacted with organic compounds and metal ions, resulting in their exploitation in remediation of wastewater (Z. Feng et al., 2019; Z. Feng, Odelius and Hakkarainen, 2018; Z. Feng, Simeone, Odelius and Hakkarainen, 2017; Guo et al., 2020).

Generally, the chemical and physical properties, like the type and abundancy of the chemical functionality and morphologies, and subsequently the material characters are the key factors for the successive applicability of CQDs. The role of synthetic technique is therefore crucial. The investigated methodologies for synthesizing of CQDs can be categorized into two divisions, ascribed as “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches. Top-down approach ascribed as decomposition of the bulky carbon-based materials like graphene, oxide graphite, carbon nanotubes into the nano-clustered materials with particle size lesser than ten nm through arc discharge (X. Xu et al., 2004), laser ablation (Sun et al., 2006), and electrochemical methods (Deng et al., 2014). Bottom-up approaches are proceeded for synthesis of CQDs from small organic precursors or organic polymeric matrix with the merits of being appropriate for large-scaled productions, with different of CQDs precursor molecules and controllable compositions and characters. This could involve the dehydration, repolymerization and aromatization steps through hydrothermal or basic/acidic oxidation (Y. Hu, Yang, Tian, Jia and Yu, 2014), microwave assistance (Q. Wang, Liu et al., 2012), plasma treatment (X. Ma et al., 2020), and thermal decomposition techniques (Xue et al., 2016). More recently, some reports were considered with investigation of new techniques for increment of the sustainability through the nucleation of more in-lines considered with green chemistry concepts. So far, this widely centered on exploitation of green carbon resources i.e., biomass-based materials and progression of greener synthetic processing.

Recently, numerous synthetic techniques were investigated for nucleation of CQDs, which could be categorized as bottom-up and top-down methodologies (Fig. 1 ) (Bao et al., 2011; Deng et al., 2014; W. Li et al., 2018; W. Li et al., 2021; Yuan Liu et al., 2020; H. Song et al., 2021; H. Wu et al., 2021). Unfortunately, various recent techniques required toxic chemicals, elevated temperature, long reaction duration, and complex steps. In addition to, top-down methodologies were reported to be mainly dependent on non-renewable or highly costed carbon resources were usually required. Therefore, nucleation of fluorescent CQDs via a facile, low cost, and sustainable route was highly interested. In some of recent reports, fabrication of CQDs via integration of CQDs into suitable polymeric matrices through the intermolecular interaction or covalent bonding could be ascribed as feasible pathways. Correspondingly, for different applications, various organic polymers are found to compatible matrices.

Compatible polymeric building blocks could play a vital role in stably anchoring of CQDs to efficiently prevent the agglomeration-motivated photoluminescence self-quenching of CQDs (Y. Zhou, Sharma, Peng and Leblanc, 2017). Moreover, the achievement of this concept might control the nanocomposites macro-morphological features and acquires the product some advantageous properties like enhanced self-supporting ability, portability, thermal conductivity or rapid electron and enhanced chemical stabilities for CQDs. Meaning that, the characteristic properties of CQDs could grant the polymeric matrix with other potential capability like the promoted mechanical strengthening, excellence in the fluorescence potency, the ionic conductivities, bioactivities, self-healing, and stimuli-response character (Goh et al., 2012; Konwar et al., 2015; Yamin Liu et al., 2016; C. Ma et al., 2018; S. Wu et al., 2020; C. Zhu et al., 2017). Such multi-functionalized CQDs/polymer composites characterized with broad applications in different purposes (Fig. 2 ), differing from the portable contaminants detecting reagents (Q. Wu et al., 2018), adsorbing reagents (Z. Feng et al., 2019), batteries (C. Ma et al., 2018), super-capacity laborers (Oskueyan et al., 2019), solar cells (H. B. Yang et al., 2013), medical scaffold (D. Wu, Bäckström and Hakkarainen, 2017), and drug carrying templates (Singh et al., 2017), in addition to their superior applicability as anti-tumor and biocidal reagents for biomedical purposes (Ahmed et al., 2021; Emam and Ahmed, 2021; Emam et al., 2021). By comparing with the other types of CQDs/polymeric composites like graphene oxide, graphene and carbon nanotubes polymeric composites, the advantageous character of CQDs/polymer composites lied in, the varieties in CQDs precursors, in addition to biocompatible resources, wide ranges of biomass starting materials like, agricultural and industrial byproducts could be utilized. Additionally, CQDs synthetic methodologies are milder, the superior optical characters of CQDs, endowing the composites with different applications in the environmental and medical purposes, the controllable size of CQDs resulting in the wider surface area for interaction with the polymeric matrix and CQDs significantly possess a better biocompatibility comparing with to the carbon nanotubes.

This review is considerably represented a systematic overview about CQDs originated from polysaccharides and their applications in various chemical purposes. Some of the previously reported reviews that was focused on the summarization of the conventional synthesizing techniques of CQDs and their fundamental characters, the hypothesized mechanisms of photoluminescence and the applications related to their optical activities (Tuerhong et al., 2017; X. Wang, Feng et al., 2019; J. Zuo et al., 2015). The current approach also aimed to highlight the nucleation of CQDs from cost effective biological origins i.e., via ecofriendly/green synthetic methodologies. Furthermore, the basic concepts for the production of nanocomposites of CQDs immobilized within polysaccharide matrix (CQDs@polysaccharide nanocomposites) are represented, while, it is worthily approved that the composition of the nanocomposites containing biologically originated CQDs and eco-friendly polysaccharide/bio-decomposable polymers are especially presented. Additionally, the recent investigation of some cutting-edge application of the CQDs/polysaccharide nanocomposites in the field of biomedicine is also discussed. Eventually, the future perspectives and the recent challenges in such topic are also provided in order to open up innovative angles for the exploitation of such green approaches for fabrication of CQD/polysaccharide nanocomposites as well as widening their application scopes.

Taking in account the selection of appropriate carbon precursor molecules for synthesis of CQDs, biological masses have unique advantages like low cost, nontoxicity, renewability, and wide availability. Exploitation of biological masses were supported the bioeconomy-an economy modelling where the basic building units for chemicals and energy are originated from renewable/biocompatible resources (Duquenne et al., 2020). Replacing of the conventional precursor molecules with biological resources is a key factor forward, but until the elevated temperatures or long durations could diminish the sustainability. However, CQDs with variable structures and characters could be produced owing to the diversities of natural biological masses.

Polysaccharides are one of the common diverse and important type of biological molecules and represent well-identified chiral scaffolds that motivated the researchers to investigate different techniques for modification the anomeric positions and the alcoholic accessibilities. Thus, the exploitation of polysaccharides as starting equivalents for synthesis of CQDs is extensively considerable not only owing to their abundancy, availabilities and heterogeneities, but also attributing to their high hydrophilicity, low-carbonization temperature, cost effectiveness and nontoxicity (Table 1 ). Considering with all the as-mentioned options for tuning the synthetic technique of CQDs, it is not surprisingly that researchers have already started to study and approve the beneficial characters of polysaccharides by focusing on synthesizing of new Fluorescence active CQDs with improved characters. Polysaccharides are basically polymeric matrices build from monosaccharaides bonded together via glycosidic links to give long branched or linear molecular chains. Some of the commonly applicable carbohydrates were exploited for synthesis of CQDs, like arabic gum (Ahmed and Emam, 2020), pullulan, carrageenan (Emam and Ahmed, 2021) and carboxymethyl cellulose (Ahmed et al., 2021).

Additionally, chitin, cellulose, glycogen and starch were also exploited in clustering of CQDs (Shi, 2016). With hydrolysis, these materials could be broken down into smaller fragmented molecules of oligosaccharides or monosaccharaides. Therefore, it is not surprising that such natural polymers were also exploited as CQDs precursor molecules. Many CQDs synthetic techniques were reported for the functionalization of biological masses, specially originated from the plants, which are mainly a huge origin of naturally generated polymers accompanied with smaller amounts of other organic compounds, like amino acids, that could act as dopant reagents. Special examples including the application of orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), garlic (Zhao et al., 2015), onion wastes (Bandi et al., 2016) and general kitchen wastes (Himaja et al., 2014) were also presented in literature. Chen et al. reported a considerable study for preparation of CQDs via hydrothermal technique with particle size of 2.3–3.5 nm originated from starch macromolecule, to be applied in bioimaging (W. Chen et al., 2018). According to Ding et al. CQDs (2–6 nm) could be ingrained from lignin macromolecules to be also applied in bioimaging (Ding et al., 2018). While, Wei et al. reported that CQDs clustered form starch macromolecules could be successfully exploited in detection of Cu+2 (Wei et al., 2014). While, cellulosic materials were also studied for their superiority in nucleation of CQDs (2–6 nm) to be also applied in detection of copper ions (Yingshuai Liu, Zhao and Zhang, 2014).

Pramanik et al. also were exploited the hypothesis for the multi-functionality of polysaccharides that containing different accessible functional groups in synthesizing of CQDs from three different carbohydrate polymers; chitosan (Chi-CQDs), alginic acid (Alg-CQDs) and starch (S-CQDs) with PEG-200 using microwave-assisted synthetic methodology (Chandra et al., 2012). Transmission electron microscopic analysis of the samples was approved that a range of topographical features and size distributions were obtained dependent upon the polymers applied. For example, S-CQDs were represented with the smallest average size of 1–2 nm but few of morphological uniformities. Additionally, Chi-CQDs appeared to exhibit a distinctly spherical feature with a size distribution of 2–10 nm and Alg-CQDs also were exhibited with a distinct spherical geometry with a size distribution of 2–4 nm. Considerably, an inversely relation between the size distribution of CQDs and the photoluminescence output was achieved, the smallest S-CQDs produced the best photoluminescence intensity of the samples, while the largest Chi-CQDs had the lowest intense. FTIR spectral data gave an evidence that the starting carbohydrate polymer functional groups are reflected onto CQDs.

Alginic acid exhibited with one carboxylic group for each monomeric unit, whereas chitosan is an aminated polysaccharide; the data of the different CQDs exhibited higher intensity for bands owing to carboxylic acid group (carbonyl bonds) in both Alg-CQDs and Chi-CQDs. Similarly, Chi-CQDs exhibited abundance of amine functionalities, while S-CQDs spectral data showed many bands which can be referred to alcoholic groups and some carboxylic acid functional groups, with the latter probably produced through the interaction. The researchers further represented the application of the different compounds in sensing of heavy metals. Therefore, each CQDs sample was exposed to 0.001 M of Cu2+, Cd2+, Sn2+ or Zn2+ in reaction liquor and the optical responses were monitored. S-CQDs exhibited considerable optical responses, in the case of Cu2+, a significant diminishing in optical property was estimated. The researchers reported that, owing to its paramagnetism, Cu2+ can quench S-CQDs fluorescence through a photo-motivated electron transferring mechanism, in which, Cu2+ is reduced to Cu+. The presence of Cu+ was affirmed by the selected area of electron diffraction (SAED) in transmission electron microscope data of CQDs.

CQDs from hydrogels of chitosan were also presented by Chowdhury et al. (Chowdhury et al., 2012). Hydrogels were prepared from mixing of acetic acid and glycerol with chitosan, as highly stable starting equivalent for CQDs. Microwave assisted technique for production of hydrogel resulted in yielding of hydrogels with ultraviolet-blue emitted CQDs with size distribution of 0.6–8.7 nm. Results of Zeta-potential showed that, the produced CQDs yielded a value of +27 mV, indicating the abundance of amino functional groups as expected from chitosan as starting material containing amino groups. In addition to, these groups were also investigated for CQDs synthesized from chitosan/silver and chitosan/gold nanocomposites, which were impregnated while hydrogels-based chitosan were synthesized. It was notified that; despite of emitting of new CQDs was wide scaled and lesser well-defined, there was an improvement in photoluminescence emission for silver or gold-doped CQDs. Consequently, during 2014, the same authors were capable to show that uploading of chitosan hydrogel-based CQDs on the calcium alginate (CA) pearls produced a new nanostructure that can be exploited as pH-responsive drug-delivery template (N. Gogoi and Chowdhury, 2014).

CQDs were applied as a capping layer onto calcium alginate beads and tetracycline (TC) was loaded onto CQD–CA pearls. It was represented that a two-fold increment on the drug uploading was shown by comparing with uncoated pearls. Consequently, TC releasing at wide pH range was followed up to 96 h and it was shown that seventy percent of TC was released at pH 1 by comparing with thirty six percent of releasing was obtained at pH 7 and twenty seven percent at pH twelve.

In order to enhance the efficiency of the complex in drug delivery, the researchers were investigated a unique complex ascribed as β-cyclodextrin/tetracycline (β-TC) as a host–guest inclusion complex, that promotes a second “barrier” for releasing. The pre-designed β-TC complex was uploaded on CQDs–CA pearls with high loading levels of 90% and slower rate of TC releasing at different pH values, as expected from a higher stabilized drug uploaded within nanocomplex (drug@nanocomplex). The data revealed that the prepared complex could be expressed as good example of the potential applicability of amine-uploaded CQDs as superior templates in drug-delivery purposes. Chitin, is cheap, readily abundant type of linear polysaccharides composed of β-1,4-bonded N-acetyl-d-glucosamine building units, and is the second most abundant biological polymer that mainly composed the backbone of the insects and the crustaceans exoskeleton and is also existed in the yeast and fungi cell walls (Rinaudo, 2006). Chitin is also the chitosan precursor that is notoriously insoluble in water, and is prepared via the N-deacetylation reaction of the partially free NH2 groups. Shchipunov et al. firstly innovated the synthesis of CQDs in de-ionized water via the hydrothermal technique from chitin macromolecules in the presence of nitric acid (Shchipunov et al., 2015). Centrifugation, filtration and dialysis steps were performed for purification of the clustered CQDs from the unreacted chitin. Under ultraviolet excitation, nitrogen-doped CQDs were exhibited by blue-emission and stabilized fluorescence. These findings could suppose other opportunities in such purpose for this type of water insoluble nitrogen-containing polysaccharide.

Hyaluronic acid is another type of nitrogen-based polysaccharide composed of glucuronic acid and N-acetyl-d-glucosamine, forming the core portion of the complex proteoglycan agglomerations that are found in the extracellular matrices (Rinaudo, 2006). Du Shao et al. investigated another method for preparation of CQDs from nitrogen-doped hyaluronic acid and studied their applicability as drug delivery template (M. Zhang et al., 2016), while, hyaluronate applied for nucleation of CQDs in the presence of glycine, to yield CQDs with particle size of 10 nm. The instrumental analysis of the prepared CQDs affirmed the existence of CO based accessible groups such as COOH and CONH2, which capped a graphitic core. The prepared CQDs were shown with blue-emission under ultraviolet excitation but green when excited at 496 nm and exhibited with excitation-dependent emission. Additionally, the authors suggested that, owing to the polymeric nature of the starting equivalent, the produced nitrogen-doped CQDs cores could be decorated by unreacted hyaluronic acid fragments (CQDs decorated with unreacted hyaluronic acid; HA–CQDs). Consequent cell feeding procedures with HA–CQDs with U251 and HeLa cells, revealed that, upon internalization of CQDs, it could be localized within the cell cytoplasm and especially around the nucleus. Owing to the high amounts of internalization, receptor-mediated endocytosis was hypothesized. Particles were applied as optical probes for targeting CD44 high expressing in cancer cells, for opening the doors to this category of carbohydrate-based nanostructure for other drug-delivery, living cell labelling, and imaging applications.

In addition to nitrogen-containing carbohydrate polymers, another neutral polysaccharide-based polymeric matrix was also exploited in the synthesis of CQDs. Cyclodextrin is a cyclic glucose based polymeric structure which is mainly existed in three forms of alpha (with six glucose units in each chain), beta and gamma (with seven glucose units in each chain). Wang et al. were represented another method for synthesis of CQDs from the three mentioned types of cyclodextrin through hydrothermal technique under acidic conditions at 70 °C for 4 h (M. Hu et al., 2014). The interaction of each type of cyclodextrin yielded quasi-spherical nanoclusters with an amorphous carbon core and a size distribution of 2.5 ± 0.8 nm. The obtained CQDs exhibited with a characteristic chemical composition with decorative OH and CO containing functional groups presented on their outer surface. The quantum yields were estimated to be ranged in 9–13%, that were depended on the type of cyclodextrin exploited in clustering, with each CQDs to show green emitting light under ultraviolet radiation and excitation-independent emission from 360 to 460 nm of excitation, that is characteristic for uniform morphology. The researchers hypothesized that the characteristic emission could be owing to either the controllable size or the regulated surface state, producing a single CQDs emission profiling. This considerable report highlighted the concept that reducing sugars are not essential for nucleation of CQDs and under the acidic conditions and motivation conditions, the exploited polysaccharides could undergo the acetal hydrolyzing, dehydrating, aromatizing and carbonization reactions to produce the desirable CQDs.

The prepared cyclodextrins derived CQDs were then applied for detecting the concentration of silver ions in aqueous medium. It was initially shown that mixing of silver nitrate in a colloidal solution of CQDs under the effect of sunlight leaded to formal reducing of silver ions to elemental silver particles, that was supposed to take a place through adhering of silver ions to the CQDs outer surface, sequenced by reduction reaction under the effect of sunlight, that promoted the excitation of the reducing electrons to higher energy state. With ultraviolet–visible absorption and transmission electron microscopic measurements, it was proposed that the outer surface layer of the plasmonic silver presented on their surface. Photoluminescence intensities of CQDs were shown in a linear relationship with silver ions concentration, and CQDs might be utilized as optically active probes for detection of silver ions in aqueous medium for concentrations up to 0–25 μM.

Beta-Cyclodextrin was also utilized for synthesizing of CQDs with surface passivation and inorganic dehydration methodology. Yang and Teo et al. presented that synthesizing of green emissive CQDs as excitation independent structures can be realized under thermolysis conditions via the interaction of beta-cyclodextrin using OEI (oligo-ethyleneimine) and H3PO4 (C. Yang, Thomsen, Ogaki, Kjems and Teo, 2015). The authors affirmed that, exploitation of phosphoric acid was vital for the successive nucleation of optically active CQDs. Transmission electron microscopic analysis approved that quasi-spherical CQDs with particle size of 2–4 nm were successfully clustered via the demonstrated technique, while, data of FTIR and XPS showed that NO2 groups were decorated the outer surface of the produced CQDs. Green-emissive CQDs were photo-stabilized at pH range of 1–13 and with long-exposing to the excitation source. The obtained results revealed the advantageous properties of inorganic-ion mediated dehydrating process and the exploitation of nitrogen-doping through the surface passivation to obtain quantum yield percent of 30%. Owing to the application of oligo-ethyleneimine, CQDs were positively charged as estimated by zetapotential, resulting in nucleation of unique nanostructures that could compose nano-complexes with negatively charged polymer matrices. For demonstration of their accessibility, CQDs were decorated with HA (hyaluronic acid), a negatively-charged carbohydrate polymer, and affirmed via TEM, DLS and AFM, to be clustered as nano-agglomerates with size up to 250 nm. Considerably, the emissive characters of CQDs were not changed with complexing to hyaluronic acid. The produced nano-agglomerates were then uploaded with DOX (doxorubicin) and a strong relationship between the dose and cell mortality was studied for lung cancer H1299 cells.

Cellulose as the most abundant organic polymer on the world with a linear polymeric shape composed of repeating beta-1,4-bonded glucose molecules. Similar to cyclodextrin, cellulose is not containing nitrogen based functional groups that were shown to be important for nucleation of photoluminescent CQDs. For exploitation of the abundancy, renewable and cost effectiveness advantageous property demonstrated via the application of cellulose as the starting equivalent, nitrogen-doping strategy is required. More recently, Yao ascribed the production of CQDs from cellulose via the hydrothermal technique using urea (Shen et al., 2016). The produced CQDs were blue-green emissive with excitation-correlated emission and quantum yield of twenty-one percentage. High quantum yield and preferable optical properties were partially owing to auxochrome nitrogen containing accessible groups within the construction of CQDs. Consequent CQDs insertion procedures in MC3T3 osteoblast cells revealed that after exposing up to 24 h, cell viability was not changed by using CQDs up to 250 μg/mL. LCSM procedures affirmed that CQDs were readily inserted into the cells and can potentially be applicable in the drug-delivery purpose.

As a unique category of nanostructures, CQDs were elicited extensive consideration in the last decade. From crucial perspectives to properties and applications, photoluminescence is the most remarkable characterization of CQDs. CQDs possess characteristic optical activities that could reflect impacts from nanoparticles of differing in size distribution in the same sample (Ahmed et al., 2021; Ahmed and Emam, 2020; Emam and Ahmed, 2021; Emam et al., 2021). Additionally, different emissive sites are originated from each CQD. However, discovering of the optical activities of small/shape regulated CQDs are dubious owing to authorized mechanism of photoluminescence is still not clear. One exceptional character of the photoluminescence of CQDs is the clear excitation wavelength-dependence of the wavelength and intensity of emission. Using of surfactant-treated silica spheres as resols and carriers as carbon precursor molecules, CQDs with particle size of 1.5–2.5 nm were prepared by surface passivation with PEG1500N (polyethylene glycol). The obtained quantum yield of the passivized CQDs was estimated to be 14.7%. A dispersion of passivated CQDs exhibited strong blue emission by exciting at 365 nm. These CQDs have broad emission spectral data, to be extended from 430 to 580 nm, and they exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission photoluminescence. Formidable and vibrant photoluminescence of CQDs could be described to the existence of surface energy trapping due to surface passivation (P. Zuo, Lu, Sun, Guo and He, 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodistribution and pharmaco-kinetic studies of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Intravenous (i.v.) and subcutaneous (s.c.) injecting are the main techniques for CQDs administration. While, such CQDs diffused the systematic circulation, they identified the targeted organ and binding to it. After binding to the targeted site, irradiation is emitted by each of CQDs. Color of the photoluminescence is mainly depending on the particle size of the CQDs and could be easily estimated and identified by different methods. CQDs, are small semiconductor nanostructures with particle size of 1–10 nm produced with various surface passivation processing either by functionalizing or chemical treatment. These have low toxicity and high optical activity, and thus have different applicability in drug delivery, bioanalysis, bioimaging and other related purposes. Moreover, it is necessary to consider the bio-saving researching reports of CQDs, that including the biodistribution and pharmacokinetic studies. Yang et al. were firstly investigated the biodistribution processing of CQDs by combination of the whole-body imaging and 13C labeling (S.-T. Yang et al., 2009). Translocation and biodistribution of CQDs in animals were concluded. It was reported that, CQDs cannot pass through blood brain barrier, but can superiorly disperse to the whole body. Moderate accumulation was detected in specific sites and organs such as the kidneys, liver and spleen (N. Li et al., 2012; Tao et al., 2012).

Yang et al. and Tao et al. conducted the toxicity and biodistribution studying reports via labeling the CQDs using I125. Analysis of Pharmacokinetics for CQDs was carried out using the two-compartment modelling. The half-life time for distribution of CQDs was 0.1 h and the half-life time of clearance was 2.1 h (Tao et al., 2012; S.-T. Yang et al., 2009). Distribution patterns of CQDs were closer to that reported by Yang et al. as moderate aggregation was shown in the spleen, liver, kidneys, but totally not observed in the brain. Studies of biodistribution of CQDs were also performed by Li et al. in mice using intravenous injection. Bioimaging of dissected and sliced sites was carried out under excitation wavelength 405 nm and 500 nm emission wavelength (D. Wu, A. Samanta et al., 2017). Blue photoluminescence was estimated in various organs like in spleen, liver, heart, kidney, lungs, small intestine, and brain. High concentrations of CQDs were observed in spleen. In other exposing pathways, owing to high lipophobicity and controllable size, CQDs exhibited free translocation in the whole body. Biodistribution of Gd-CQDs was discovered by Xu et al. after intra-tracheal instillation. Quantification of Gd3+ was carried out so as to estimate the biodistribution of CQDs (Y. Xu, Jia, Yin, He and Zhang, 2014). Aggregation of CQDs in liver, kidney, lungs, heart, and spleen was approved. Cancer-bearing mice mostly had the same distributions with an aggregation of ten percentage injected doses per gram of tissue (ID g−1) in the cancer. The researchers stated that CQDs could freely translocate as well as disperse in different sites of the whole body (C. Chen and Wang, 2016).

Ability of some chemicals or mediator cells for destroying the living cells is known as cytotoxicity. Such chemicals could induce the accidental cell death (necrosis) or programmed cell death (apoptosis) in healthy cells of animals and human beings. CQDs were considered as possible replacement for metallic quantum dots and dyes in bioimaging owing to their chemical stabilities, broad ranges of excitation, and superior optical activities. Biocompatibility is importantly considerable for their applicability in cell labeling and bioimaging thus leading them the most essential property of CQDs. The cytotoxicity of CQDs is expressed as main concern. Studying reports related to the cytotoxicity of CQDs were conducted via different scientific researchers, organizations, universities and institutes, and satisfied data and researching reports were presented.

Zhang et al. were investigated the cytotoxic effects of CQDs on mesangial cells of rats (Z. Zhang, Duan, Yu, Yan and Chen, 2015). No apparent cytotoxic effects and much better bio-saving characters of CQDs were reported for bioactive probing applications. Optically active CQDs were prepared and evaluated for cytotoxic effects (X. Zhang et al., 2013). Different key parameters like cell viability, malondialdehyde, ROS (total reactive oxygen species), glutathione, and lactate dehydrogenase were estimated via 16HBE (human bronchial epithelial) cell line. The data approved that CQDs were significantly enhanced the permeability for the membrane of 16HBE cells. CQDs were induced the oxidative stress, that exhausted the antioxidation defensing of cells, resulting in the decrement of the cell viability. Therefore, the surface decoration of CQDs could diminish their cytotoxic impacts (X. Zhang et al., 2013).

Sun et al. studied in vitro and in vivo cytotoxic effects of CQDs via the detection of cell mortality and proliferation of human breast cells (MCF-7 cells) and human colorectal adenocarcinoma cells (HT-29 cells) that were estimated via conduction of the trypan blue and methylthiazol tetrazolium assays after exposure to CQDs (Sun et al., 2006). The applied CQDs were prepared via PEG1500N laser ablating and surface passivation methodologies. CQDs as reagents were functionalized in vivo examination in mice. CQDs were given a bright photoluminescence appearance in aqueous form. No serious toxicological responses were reported when CQDs colloids were taken intravenous in mice. These CQDs were get out from the body primarily in the urine within nearly 3 h of injection, thus identifying the nontoxic nature of CQDs on both of the tested cells (S.-T. Yang et al., 2009).

Fluorescence of CQDs in the kidneys and liver could be estimated after 4 h of intravenous injection. High aggregation of CQDs resulted in higher optical activities in the kidneys by comparing with that detected in the liver. Excretion in urine is expressed as the chief pathways for outgoing of CQDs, also results in higher optical activity. No toxic effects were reported in case of intravenous administration of CQDs in the male CD-1 mice. Also, after exposing to CQDs for twenty-eight days represented no toxicity impacts (S.-T. Yang et al., 2009). From all of the above-mentioned researching reports, it could be concluded that CQDs have considerable adequacy for in vitro and in vivo drug delivery and bioimaging purposes. Numerous researching studies were supposed that CQDs could exhibit biocompatibility almost closer to that of United States Food and Drug Administration-affirmed for indocyanine green as an organic dye applicable for the optical bioimaging. In spite of, more studies considered with the cytotoxic effects of CQDs are required, the above-mentioned reports could hypothesize a bright future for CQDs in various biomedical purposes.

CQDs were numerously reported for its superiority for application in biomedical purposes owing to its bactericidal, viricidal and antitumor potentiality (Fig. 3 ), as it exhibits advantageous properties that could be pointed out as follows; i) cost effectiveness and abundancy, that leading them a unique member in the nanostructures family; ii) photostability compared with other conventional QDs and organic dyes; iii) narrow emission and broader excitation than other cadmium-based QDs and organic dyes, iv) superior biological properties such as lipophobicity, low toxic effects, chemical stability, and high biocompatibility approve their promising applicability in biosensing, drug delivery, and bioimaging; v) great photoluminescence compared with other QDs, vi) high stability in aqueous medium compared to the organic dyes and other cadmium-based QDs, vii) outstanding electronic properties as electron donating and accepting reagents causing the chemiluminescence and electrochemical luminescence, empowering them with wide potential in optronics, catalyzing, and sensing purposes and vii) very high chemical inertness compared to other traditional or metallic QDs (Tope et al., 2014).

It was highly motivated in utilization CQDs as substituents for other toxic inorganic QDs especially applicable in bioimaging (S.-T. Yang et al., 2009). The optical characters of CQDs are advantageously regarded with the broad excitation spectral mapping, different emissive spectral data and high photo-stabilization. In addition to, they exhibited high biocompatibility and low cytotoxic effects. Other important characters of CQDs and biopolymeric matrices had further enabled the investigative developing of the smart composites with novel or improved characters, like enhanced mechanical characters of the polymers.

It was an incentive times to save the photoluminescence of CQDs when impregnated in the polymer matrices for biomedical purposes. The photoluminescence of CQDs@polymer composites is essentially required for enabling the sensing of biomolecular structures and proteins, and transfecting biopolymeric matrices with simultaneous bioimaging. Sensing of biomolecular structures like glucose is of significant values for managing of diabetes. Glucose imprinted poly (N-isopropylacrylamide-acrylamide-vinylphenylboronic acid) copolymeric microgels superiorly provided that the assisted converting of biochemical peaks to optical peaks with assisting of CQDs (Wang et al., 2015). Microgels could be shrunk and swelled depending on glucose concentrations, that resulting in recovering and quenching of the optical signals. Sensing of other small biomolecular structures and proteins was further progressed via the bioelectrodes based on CQDs/polymeric composites (Gao et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2013). In this category the optical properties of CQDs were not required, but adding of CQDs could enhance the interaction with the sensors and the electron transferring between sensing interfaces and electrode, respectively.

Sensors of dopamine was obtained via the exploitation of chitosan composite of gold coated with CQDs (Au@CQDs chitosan composite) modified with glassy electrode (Huang et al., 2014), while, CQDs enabled the reaction with dopamine owing to their -ve charges and gold leaded to better conductivities. Au@CQDs chitosan composites can further prevent the background current from uric acid and ascorbic acid. Assessing of the overexpression of histone can be bonded to different acute diseases like dengue. For histone photometrical detection, a quarternized CQDs/deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) biological nano-hybrid was investigated (Maiti et al., 2013). With histone, DNA would favorably react with histone instead of the quarternized CQDs. Consequently, the optical signal of quarternized CQDs improved, that enabled the estimation of histone.

Another smart sensing objects exfoliating CQDs were further investigated (D.-Y. Li et al., 2016). The designed polymeric based composites based on thermosensitive poly-propyl-acrylamide (PNIPAM) with silica uploaded with CQDs were impregnated with nonpeptides for identifying the protein Cytochrome C. Above LCST (the lower critical solution temperature), that is nearly 32 °C, PNIPAM underwent swelling, that was utilized in order to recognize and release the cytochrome C. While, PNIPAM exhibited a higher fluorescence band and acted as a random lipophobic coils with optimum conditions for relinking of the protein, below LCST. Designing of multifunctional nano-supporting templates as carriers has demonstrated to be promising as a diagnostic tool and for gene therapy.

Inorganic QDs and Organic dyes can be applied for labeling of plasmid DNA and the polymeric vectors but were shown health concerning and instability, respectively. CQDs coated with polyethylenimine network provided higher transfecting of plasmid DNA into HepG2and COS-7 cells compared with polyethylenimine alone (C. Liu et al., 2012). Intriguingly, the prepared hydrogel exhibited a capability for simultaneous bioimaging with differing the wavelength of excitation that held to be promising for in vivo bioimaging under near IR emissions. Real-time following up of gene delivery was further showed by Au@CQDs complexed with polyethylenimine and unlabeled plasmid DNA (Kim et al., 2013). High salt concentrations simulated the dissociation of the system with recoveree of the photoluminescence signal.

CQDs with surface lipophobic character are a prerequisite functionality for fabricating of hydrogels in combining with the hydrophilicity of the polymers. The hydrophilicity of CQDs is correlated to the decorative functional groups on the surface, such like, O–H, COOH and O–C–O, which are also served for high water dispersibility and low cytotoxic effects. The functionality of CQDs might be interchanged to some extent via the exploitation of various precursor molecules and synthetic methodologies (L. Zhou, He and Huang, 2013). Post-treatment steps were further reported to be useful for changing the oxygen contents of CQDs (Benyahia Erdal, Adolfsson, Pettersson and Hakkarainen, 2018). Hydrogels are ascribed as soft materials that could be superiorly applicable in different biomedical purposes, but the mechanical characters are too poor for the repetitive using.

Utilization of CQDs in the modification of PAM (polyacrylamide) as a chemically crosslinked hydrogel, was provided advantageous mechanical characters (M. Hu et al., 2016). In this approach, a stretchable hydrogel with the elongation percent of 3700 was successfully prepared, while, unzipping of physical crosslinking between polyacrylamide and carbon quantum dots was hypothesized to be proceeded, in addition to high fracture strengthening of 166 kPa was realized. Moreover, additional hydrogel was prepared from PAM that was reported to exhibit a blue emission by compositing with CQDs, that also aided in the improvement of the mechanical properties (Y.-Q. Wang et al., 2017). For instance, the addition of CQDs with weight percentage of 0.67% leaded to increase of the mechanical strengthening from 112 kPa to 161 kPa and the elongation percentage from 676 to 842 of the networked hydrogel.

CQDs treated hydrogels are reported to be promisingly applicable as thermo-responsive drug delivery systems with simultaneous bioimaging, as CQDs are identified to be capable for holding the drugs via the intermolecular forces to be exhibited by photoluminescence under ultraviolet/visible irradiation. CQDs were impregnated within the network of PAM with PNIPAM as thermo-responsive polymer to obtain smart hydrogel that exhibited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation at ambient conditions (L. Zhou et al., 2013). Photoluminescence intensities of the prepared hydrogels were shown to behave as a temperature-dependent feature and were also demonstrated to be highly reversible. Above LCST (the lower critical solution temperature), a collapsed system and agglomerated CQDs were given rise for PNIPAM, which diminished the photoluminescence intensities. The same principle with tunable photoluminescence was superiorly adapted for a covalently CQDs@PNIPAM networked hydrogel at a wide-ranged of concentrations of CQDs (P. Zhang et al., 2012).

Moreover, targeted specific hydrogels were fabricated from conjugates of hyaluronic acid and CQDs (Goh et al., 2012). Analysis of In vivo approved the successful delivery of the hyaluronic acid@CQDs hydrogels to hyaluronic acid receptors in the liver cells with CQDs as bioimaging reagents. The capability of drug delivery for CQDs@chitosan hydrogels was also monitored for 5-fluorouracil as the anticancer drug (Sachdev et al., 2016). Drug uploaded upon CQDs@chitosan hydrogel was diminished the cell viability percentage estimated by MTT assay for A549 and NIH 3 T3 cells after two days. A549 cells were examined for bioimaging that was proceeded after adding of CQDs@hydrogel uploaded with and without 5-fluorouracil. Green color that was emitted from the applicable hydrogel, could be exploited for differentiation between apoptotic and living cells.

Drug delivery of Dox (doxorubicin) as anticancer drug was achieved by releasing of the drug in acidic medium via the functionalization of CQDs immobilized within DNA hydrogel (CQDs@DNA hydrogel) (Singh et al., 2017). Cell viability percentage for HeLa cells was also approved to be diminished to 65% via contacting with doxorubicin-uploaded CQDs@DNA hydrogel. Additionally, CQDs coated with PEG impregnated within chitosan in hybrid hydrogel (PEG@chitosan@CQDs hybrid hydrogel) as pH-sensible template with optical sensing was synthesized (Wang et al., 2015). Hydrogel was also characterized by near IR dual response delivering for lipophilic anticancer drugs. Arrangement of CQDs immobilized within polymer (CQDs@polymer) was reported to exhibit multi-stimuli-responsive characters towards organic molecules, temperature, inorganic ions and pH (Yin et al., 2013). Arranging of hyperbranched PEI (polyethylenimine) composited with CQDs was designed via one-pot microwave interaction, which was further protected with iso-butyric amide. Via helping of CQDs, the compounds had the potentiality to be uploaded into the template that served for the purpose of drug delivery with bioimaging characters (Fig. 4 ).

Additional highly considerable biomedical application for CQDs is the bone tissue engineering that is mainly serving in reconstruction of defects in the bones. The main components of the bone tissue are the collagen and hydroxyapatite. For regeneration of the bone tissues, scaffolds are essential for the structural integrities, which could provide adhesive forces for the cells, differentiation and proliferation. After inserting, the scaffolds should be resorbed into the harmless molecules after a considerable period of time needed for healing has crossed away. There were different polymeric matrices that could be served in this purpose but they were unfortunately lacking the biological activity that is aiding for production of the new bone tissues. CQDs were reported to be capable for assisting the osteoblastic cells for growing on waterborne/biodegradable/hyperbranched polyurethane scaffolds (Fig. 5 ) (S. Gogoi, Kumar, Mandal and Karak, 2015). In-situ impregnation of CQDs within polymerizing of the thermosetting further obtained an improved mechanical potency in terms of elongation at break, strengthening and toughness that also can be improved via the increment of CQDs concentrations from 0.5 to 1 and 1.5 wt percentage.

Carbon based nanofillers were also exhibited by biological activity and could facilitate the synthesis of hydroxyapatite, like, the hydroxyapatite crystals on polymeric materials. CQDs are shown to be promisingly applicable for these purposes owing to their nanosizing and enriching with oxygen functionality, which is thought to attract the Ca+2 ions and additionally leaded to the successive growth of hydroxyapatite. It was affirmed that addition of CQDs to PCL matrices motivated the bioactivities for biomineralizing process (Hassanzadeh et al., 2016). To enhance the mechanical potentiality of PCL matrices, CQDs were decreased in microwave technique with caffeic acid as a reducer (Erdal and Hakkarainen, 2018). At the same time, the capability to motivate biomineralization of PCL surface was assured. The mentioned approach further demonstrated that the cell viability percent of MG63 osteoblast-like cells on the reduced CQDs/PCL scaffolds was comparable to that of neat PCL scaffolds (Erdal and Hakkarainen, 2018; Yadav et al., 2019). Similarly, the bioactive polymeric matrices such as starch was shown as scaffolds for biomineralizing technique with aiding from the covalently attached CQDs (D. Wu, E. Bäckström et al., 2017). CQDs were also approved to act in improvement of the electro-spinnability for the starch fibers (D. Wu, A. Samanta et al., 2017), where the electro-spun starch@CQDs fibers simultaneously underwent decomposition. Whereas, their combinations were mimicked the construction and the functionality of the extracellular matrix as artificial interwoven fibers networks. PLA and PCL electro-spun fibers were also fabricated via immobilization of CQDs for motivation of the osteo-bioactivities (D. Wu, Samanta, Srivastava and Hakkarainen, 2018). Already, with lower uploading of CQDs (0.5–2.5%), the fibers strengthening and stiffness were reported to be enhanced by comparing with the corresponding neat fibers.

Over a decade, synthesis and the superior applicability of the optically active carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were limitedly reported in literature; however, in the last twenty years, it becomes extensively considered and numerous of researching approaches were concerned with these novel photoluminescence nanomaterials that have tremendous potentiality in different applications such as metal detection, photo-catalyzing, bioimaging and as probes for biomedical purposes, whereas, they are mainly offered as cost effective and non-toxic alternatives for the other metallic-based photoluminescent nanostructures, like, semiconductor CQDs. In the current review, the different biological-based carbon sources as precursor molecules of CQDs, photoluminescence of CQDs, biodistribution and pharmaco-kinetic studies, cytotoxicity and the biomedical applications of CQDs (Sensing and transfecting properties, Drug delivery and Bone tissue engineering) were presented. We have also demonstrated the recent reports that focused on studying the superiority of different carbohydrate as highly accessible biological macromolecules, cheap and highly abundant starting equivalents. The presence of highly accessible functional groups as decorative groups attached on the surface of the applicable CQDs were proposed to be on of the key factors that crucial for the photoluminescence properties. In addition to, the surface passivation was found to provide the uniformity of photoluminescence trapping groups on the surface of CQDs and the addition of heteroatoms as dopant reagents (electron donor), were approved to enhance and help in improvement of the photoluminescence of these extensively considered nanostructures. It was also evidenced that small tuning in the synthetic methodology functionalized for obtaining the required CQDs, has impacted on their final chemical and physical characters. Therefore, high consideration is required for the carbohydrates being applicable in synthetic technique. Despite of there is no authorized full mechanism of CQDs nucleation were elucidated till now, numerous reports were hypothesized the mechanism of the nucleation reaction of CQDs from carbohydrate polymers, were supposed that carbohydrate is decomposed via the ring-opening reaction to give aldehyde containing fragments, and subsequently followed by dehydration/aromatization, that lead to produce heteroaromatic compounds on the CQDs. The opportunities in tuning the synthetic techniques for production of CQDs synthesis were rendering these nanostructures amenable to numerous applications, as we have currently ascribed in this review.
